target: Evaluation Guide (in generate-html.js template) — new subsection 'Verdict Assignment Criteria' after 'How to Evaluate Any Scientific Claim'
insertion_point: After the </details> closing the 'eg-evaluate' section, before the 'eg-ai' section in the evaluate tab of generate-html.js
replacement_html:
Verdict Assignment Criteria

Each WIN receives the verdict matching its primary failure mode. When a WIN fails in multiple ways, the most structurally damaging failure takes priority. The six categories are not ranked by severity — they describe different kinds of failure, not degrees of it.

The six verdict categories describe qualitatively different failure modes. A WIN that contradicts the dome's own equations fails differently from one that merely restates standard physics. Assigning the correct category matters because a dome defender who can show a misclassified WIN gains a process objection against the entire review. Below we make the assignment criteria explicit so that any reader — or any AI — can audit our work.

The primary-issue rule. Most WINs exhibit multiple problems: a claim might both restate standard physics and involve data misrepresentation. We assign the verdict that captures the primary structural failure — the one that, if corrected, would still leave the claim unsupported. For example: if a WIN cites wrong numbers AND standard physics explains the observation, the primary issue is the data error (Refuted by Data) if the numbers are verifiably wrong, or standard physics (Std Model Explains) if the numbers are correct but non-discriminating. The secondary issue is noted in the detailed analysis but does not determine the verdict.

Misleading vs. Std Model Explains — the decision boundary. These two categories share the most porous border. The distinguishing question is: does the dome actively mishandle the evidence, or does it simply present a real observation that standard physics already explains?

  • Std Model Explains applies when the observation is accurately represented, the data sources are correctly cited, and the only problem is that standard physics predicts the same result without dome geometry. The dome's claim is not wrong — it is redundant. No deception is implied.
  • Misleading applies when the dome's presentation involves an additional structural problem beyond non-discrimination: cherry-picked data ranges, misrepresented source values, logically contradictory claims both counted as confirmations, the same data counted multiple times, or circular derivations where the "prediction" was fitted to the data it claims to predict. Misleading implies the evidence has been mishandled, not merely that it is unpersuasive.

Some WINs classified as Std Model Explains include secondary notes about duplication or overlap with other WINs in their detailed analysis. These notes are informational — the primary reason the WIN fails is that standard physics explains the observation, and the duplication is a compounding but not the defining issue.

Substructure within Misleading. The 22 Misleading verdicts encompass several distinct failure patterns: data misrepresentation (cited values don't match the source), count inflation (the same observation counted as multiple WINs), circular calibration (curve-fitting to known data then claiming prediction), and false-dilemma framing (citing a competitor's unsolved problem as dome evidence without offering dome-specific physics). We do not formally subdivide the category because the defining feature is the same in each case: the evidence has been actively mishandled, not merely interpreted differently. Readers who want the specific failure pattern for each WIN will find it in the point-by-point analysis in Part 3.

Self-Contradicted: the internal-consistency standard. This verdict applies only when the dome's own published equations, worked through with the dome's own stated parameters, produce a result that contradicts the dome's own claimed value. No external physics is invoked. This is the structurally strongest category because no amount of "mainstream science is wrong" can rebut it — the contradiction is between the model and itself.

On the absence of a "Confirmed" category. A careful reader will notice that no verdict category corresponds to "the dome model is correct." This is not an oversight. If we found a WIN where the dome's prediction — derived from dome geometry, not adopted from standard physics — preceded and matched an observation that standard physics could not explain, we would add such a category. We have not found one. The category's absence reflects the evidence, not an assumption.
holes_addressed: Misleading/Std Model Explains boundary not stated (HOL-TAXONOMY major hole #3)
Misleading category overloaded with four distinct failure modes (HOL-TAXONOMY major hole #2)
No positive verdict category (HOL-TAXONOMY deeper_analysis observation)
Decision criterion needs to be explicit for outside auditors
new_evidence_added: Primary-issue rule as formal decision criterion
Explicit enumeration of Misleading subtypes with rationale for not splitting
Self-Contradicted explicitly framed as needing no external physics (strongest structural category)
Transparent acknowledgment that no Confirmed category exists, with criteria for adding one

anticipated_objections.objection: Why not formally split Misleading into subcategories? 22 WINs under one label is a lot.
anticipated_objections.response: More categories create more boundaries, not fewer. The defining feature (evidence actively mishandled) is shared across all subtypes. Splitting would require re-auditing every WIN and create new boundary disputes. The point-by-point analysis in Part 3 already provides the specific failure pattern for each WIN — the verdict is the summary, not the full analysis.

anticipated_objections.objection: The primary-issue rule is subjective — who decides what the 'primary' failure is?
anticipated_objections.response: The rule provides a stated criterion that can be audited, which is better than an unstated criterion that cannot. The detailed analysis for each WIN explains why the primary issue was chosen. Readers who disagree can point to the specific reasoning they contest.

anticipated_objections.objection: The 'no Confirmed category' section admits bias — you assumed the conclusion.
anticipated_objections.response: We state the criteria for adding such a category (dome-derived prediction, preceded observation, standard physics couldn't explain). We invite readers to identify any WIN meeting these criteria. The absence of a category is a finding, not a premise. If the dome author produces a genuinely prospective prediction derived from dome geometry that standard physics cannot match, we will add the category.

anticipated_objections.objection: Some Std Model Explains WINs mention duplication — aren't those inconsistently classified?
anticipated_objections.response: The text explicitly addresses this: secondary notes about duplication are informational. The primary reason those WINs fail is non-discrimination, not duplication. The duplication language flags overlap, not the verdict-determining failure mode.

kernel_of_truth: The curmudgeon is right that the existing verdict definitions, while conceptually sound, did not state the decision rule for boundary cases. Any taxonomy with more than two categories will have boundary cases, and the absence of explicit assignment criteria invited the exact process objection the curmudgeon identified. The six categories capture genuine diversity in failure modes — but categories without stated assignment rules are just vibes.
also_recommends: Update the verdict-legend box (overview tab) Misleading definition from 'cherry-picked, duplicated, circular, or non-discriminating' to 'cherry-picked, duplicated, circular, or otherwise mishandled' — the word 'non-discriminating' in the Misleading definition blurs the boundary with Std Model Explains. Non-discriminating WINs that are otherwise accurately presented belong in Std Model Explains.

Check WIN-043 and WIN-060 finding text: both mention 'duplicate' as a secondary observation. If the primary-issue rule is adopted, their Std Model Explains verdict is correct and the duplication notes should be framed as secondary, not leading.

The Self-Contradicted tidal group (WIN-045/046/049/050/051) shares one geometric argument. Consider adding a note to the Part 2 or Part 3 introduction acknowledging that five WINs share one root cause, preempting the obvious dome-defender objection about count inflation.

verdict_definition_patch.target: Overview tab verdict-legend box, Misleading definition
verdict_definition_patch.current: The data is misrepresented, cherry-picked, the cited values do not match the actual source, or logically contradictory results are both claimed as confirmations.
verdict_definition_patch.proposed: The data is misrepresented, cherry-picked, the cited values do not match the actual source, logically contradictory results are both claimed as confirmations, or the same observation is counted as multiple independent wins.
verdict_definition_patch.rationale: Adds count-inflation (duplication) to the definition, which is already how Misleading is applied in practice. Removes implicit 'non-discriminating' from Misleading scope — those WINs belong in Std Model Explains when accurately presented.
